sentences.text: All right the-- what?
You're going to tell me about the group of people.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] Yeah, I wanted to finish, nevertheless.
I came, and I wanted to join, but I was told, no, the number is already crossed, no more accepted.
These 300 people were brought to a place outside of the city and killed.
It was only protected for this.
That was not only in Kaunas.
So it was in Riga.
So it was in VIlna So it was in all cities, to destroy the intelligent part, [INAUDIBLE] part of the population.

And then there were doctors, teachers, engineers, et cetera, you know, to work in all this. 300 people in Kaunas, which for a Jewish population is quite a lot, the same day were brought and killed-- [? liquidated. ?]
Next, I was-- I cannot work in medical.
Too many doctor are still in ghetto.
In [NON-ENGLISH], I'm not-- not [NON-ENGLISH] Kaunas.
I'm going to work every day to the airport.
Yes, that is right, with a spade, too.
We were working to enlarge the airport in-- in Kaunas.
At the beginning, for a while, I was a simple worker.
But then, you know, they realized that they need somebody to take care if something happens.
So finally, I became a doctor who-- not to work with the spade, you know-- and-- and just if somebody got wounded, you know, and needed, you know, medical help.
Plus, it is-- it was in such a shift.
Shift is correct, [? in English? ?] There were around 1,000 people.
Very few.
Oh, three-- three, it was.
The Jewish-- the Jews were speaking Jewish, were speaking--
Yiddish.
They were speaking Russian.
And they were speaking Lithuanian.
Whom?
Me?
I consider myself as very well-educated.
That was, you know-- we were-- after this-- this-- this intelligence action-- action, for a while, it was quiet.
We were going to work.
We had some rations-- not enough, always hungry.
In October-- I will not say exactly on the 13th of October or the 14th of October-- we were ordered to come to a big, empty place in this where we lived-- big empty place.
1941, October 14-- October 14th or 15th.
And then just a few Germans were there, you know, and ask us to parade, by families, one family, two families to parade.
I am with my wife.
And there was also her aunt.
And we were three persons.
33,000 people.
And right away, we're divided with the whole family with kids to the left, without kids, depends.
If it is old people, to the left.
Younger people to the right.
Germans.
And so 15,000 people were divided right away into one group. 18,000 into other group.
And we, the 15,000, were asked to go home.
The 18 were taken.
They stayed in one place.
And the next day, gathered and brought to a Tenth Fort.
I told you about Seventh Fort.
That is Tenth Fort, approximately two, three miles from our place.

And from the moment we arrived, we started to hear [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shots, screaming, terrible screaming, which is still in my ears, of this 18,000 people as they realized they are being brought here to be killed.
And [? always, ?] it lasted almost two days to kill these 18 people of Kaunas.
18,000 People.
It was a same thing done in Vilna This was the same thing done in-- in-- and on different days-- in-- in-- in Riga.
And in Riga, it was done on a Sunday.
It was done on a Sunday.
No, I wasn't there.
We didn't.
We didn't ?] [? want ?] it-- terrible.
Terrible-- we knew what is going on.
Here, I started to-- I started to realize first.
And second, I started [? to think ?] it is only for a while.
And here, you know, if we-- we were stealing the German papers, you know somehow from the-- from the-- working in the city here and there.
They will through out the newspapers.
And here, we are reading about the [GERMAN].
It is a suburb of Berlin, There was a conference without Hitler, but the top SS men.
And the Final Solution of the Jewish problem was decided.
You know what was is Final Solution?
All will [INAUDIBLE] be killed.
How I feel felt-- so I knew-- knew that I will not survive.
She felt also she will not survive.
Yes.
Not too many, but people commit suicide.
I don't recall doctors committing suicide.
But I know people committed suicide.
They knew that-- everybody realized after [GERMAN] became in the papers, that there is Final Solution of the-- of the-- the only hope was that maybe the Germans will lose the war.
But it was not a hope for this.
It is 1941.
They are proceeding.
They already took millions of Russians prisoners of war.
And the-- and the-- the Russians didn't-- didn't fight them.
Really, we didn't fight them.

After I was liberated, a colonel, a Russian colonel-- we became very friendly-- told me, absolutely, the Russians wanted to get rid of the communist regime, but the Germans didn't take any-- any difference.
They liquidated.
They took around four million Russian prisoners of war.
Of this half a million left.
Three and a half died in-- under the worst conditions in hunger-- in hunger and cold and lice.
I remained there till September '42.
In December of '41, when I was at the airport, Germans came, caught just simply 300 Jewish women, and took them away.
My wife was one of them.
After a while, I was informed-- the administration of the ghetto wanted to help me very much-- that they were taken to Riga.
My dear, we don't have any communication.
We cannot write them letters.
We cannot talk.
We cannot-- no telephone.
We are isolated-- completely isolated.
No.
I say, one day-- one day after our marriage, the Germans came.
I can say no.
There is a reason for divorce.

Divorce, you know, in the-- in the Catholic-- [INAUDIBLE] Catholic religion, when matrimonium doctorum said non consummatum, when the marriage was given, but not consumed, that is a reason for a divorce.
You understand what is consumed?
That you can say the same thing about me.
I will not say-- [INAUDIBLE] almost.
She is there.
And we didn't know.
It took a couple weeks until I was informed that she's in Riga.
And then I asked the-- the people from the administration, please, if another transport goes to Riga, I want to go with them.
I-- conditions after this were relatively quiet-- no problems, no big problems.
Sometimes they will kill a Jew who smuggled too much wood to the ghetto.
It was single-- single, you know, deaths, but not mass-- mass execution.
And so you know, I didn't want-- I didn't want to stay in ghetto.
I offered me to go as a doctor with the commander in fall, but it was July, August, to collect the-- from the fields potatoes and other vegetables.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I would [INAUDIBLE] at the head of this group.
And as a doctor of this group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few weeks later, comes one of the head from the ghetto, and say Dr.
Klebanow, we have come.
We have come to take you to the ghetto.
To the ghetto.
To the Kaunas ghetto.
I say, if you came, probably there is going again a transport to Riga.
He said, you are absolutely right.
I came back.
I became the head of this group, 300 mostly compelled, some who had relatives with women-- men who lost their wives, like me-- came voluntarily.
And so 300 Jews, they put us on the train.
And it took two or three days.
We arrived at
In Riga, I found-- you know, we were brought first to ghetto.
We stayed there for one day only.
And then we were transported.
I met my wife.
I found her.
And then we were transported to a old brewery, which is not far from the airport, because the people who have to work on the airport of-- of-- of the-- Riga.
That was called Spilwe.
Spilwe.
Two years.
That was outside, yes.
Food would we get from ghetto to Riga.
From Riga, it was-- to go to the ghetto it was approximately three or four miles-- three or four kilometers.
In Spilwe, at the beginning, 300.
Soon they started to dissolve the ghetto of Riga.
And they sent to us 800 people from Riga to our camp.
We had-- it is an old brewery.
We had plenty of space to enlarge.
And 800 people came to our ghetto.
I-- now, it was a big one thing, you know?
Shortly after-- after I-- we are in Spilwe, people are coming to me.
I see they all have temperature.
For me, I suspected that it must be either typhoid fever or paratyphoid, but of this type, it is diarrhea.
And I brought first one or two to the ghetto.
It was ghetto still at that time.
They diagnosed bacteriological that it is paratyphus.
In ghetto, they have a small hospital.
In ghetto they have--
By--
Jews.
And they told me it is paratyphoid.
And please, don't dare to bring more people to us.
I understand that if you bring, and if the Germans know, the Germans will have one only solution.
They will liquidate right away.
I-- all together, I had to know if-- right in a short time I had 20 with this-- with this sickness.
I isolated.
I-- I said it is a-- they have diarrheas.
The Germans, we had not under-- who are under control of the Luftwaffe, of the-- of the people--
--people from-- from the Air Force.
And they were very nice.
Shortly after we came, came a major.
And he-- he called me in.
And he introduced, I am [GERMAN].
I am the [GERMAN] commandant-- commandant.
And I hope that you are satisfied with the quarters what you have.
I would like to give you more food, but I get rationed that food what-- what I-- I-- I get.
I cannot get more.
But you have warm-- warm.
I supply you with-- with-- with-- with wood, no.
And I realize it is a man who is a friendly man.
He would--
My dear, you know, why-- why-- the same thing happened with this-- with this thing, you know, what-- what-- became so famous?
Schindler.
The same analog.
Two years-- although, later we were managed by the SS.
But the-- the people worked at the airport-- airport, and a still strong connection, you know, with our-- our camp.
So--
To work at the airport.
Please, for them, the airports were not big enough.
The-- right away-- [INAUDIBLE] [?
Riga ?] was the big offensive against Leningrad.
So they moved soldiers by air.
And it was rather small, so they had to enlarge.
And now, it is over 1,000 people, who were Jews, is working at the airport to enlarge the airport.
Everything going to-- to enlarge the airport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] With this, [NON-ENGLISH] with this, he [INAUDIBLE] who sent, you know, to manage us inside was [SPEAKING GERMAN]
Was sergeant--
Sergeant-- very friendly.
Yes.
And the [INAUDIBLE] was managed by Obergefreiter Schumacher-- face of a murderer, but who revealed to be a very fine man.
All major sent us sent these men, who he selected under condition that you will be good to the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doctor from-- [INAUDIBLE] medication, I had problem with medications.
Sergeant, gets up and says, let's go to the-- to the-- [GERMAN], means small hospital, the German, at the airport.
And I went there.
I got acquainted with the-- the doctor.
And the doctor even asked me-- invite-- invited me to a breakfast.
For me, it was unusual.
I was-- They addressed us not-- not the, not you-- you.
They addressed us like animals.
And here I see a completely different attitude.
My dear, every day would come approximately each morning, among the 300 in the beginning, around 15, 20, will come for these-- these complaints.
I-- I had always, you know, around 10 not working-- 10, 20 not working.
No, we came, men and women.
Yes, very many.
Not many-- few were pregnant.
And I-- right away, I was told that if a woman-- at the beginning, it was no problem.
When she's pregnant.
But soon, you know, the SS took over [? also. ?] And if, you know, SS gets informed that a woman is pregnant and gives birth to a child, she will be killed, and the child will be killed.
So I order, told, you know, please, whoever is pregnant, come.
I will terminate the pregnancy.
I'm an obstetrician gynecologist.
It's my field.
I was doing abortions.
I'm doing abortion not now for my camp.
Not far away, was so-called [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
And they were also men and women.
You know, I don't recall how many-- 500, also pregnancies-- everybody, Klebanow.
Yes, it was in this respect-- it was all the [? beginning ?] army.
So it was very fine attitude.
But I had to do-- to do.
And I will tell you, the greatest amount, it was in '44, in July.
We got 2,000 Hungarian women.
Into mine-- Spilwe.
Spilwe is a tremendous brewery.
You have places to bring them, to give them a place where to sleep.
This is the barracks.
They have straw-- straw sacks-- straw sacks.
And everybody had a [INAUDIBLE].
And among this, I had around-- I announced right away, who is pregnant to come.
I had 20 some pregnancies.
And I recall, I worked with my wife [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all night.
Doing abortions.
Saving their lives.
But one--
Yes.
Gustav Sorge was his name.
Nazi nickname-- nickname, 'the Iron Gustav.' The other--
He became commandant of our camp.
Now, when I came [INAUDIBLE] first time with a pregnancy, and I came to him and I say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hauptscharfuhrer, I examined the one, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she's pregnant.
But when I-- it came, you know-- came, you know, to termination of this pregnancy, I called him and he said, I cannot come.
I understand he was a womanizer, tremendous womanizer with the Latvian-- Latvian women.
He was very happy that he has now a gynecologist.
If something happens to these women, he will have a man who will terminate the pregnancy.
Yes.
Well, it was not [? fetal-- ?] if you have an abortion, it is usually in the first three months.
But for example, I had-- let me-- I will give you an example, a classical example.
A classic example-- a German Jewish woman from my camp, from our camp, comes to me and she says, I'm pregnant.
I examine her.
She's six months pregnant.
I say, tell me where, why did you work?
I knew already that she had some relations with the German who managed this group of people.
Not a Jew.
Floor ?] [? arbiter, ?] you know, it is-- he didn't work.
He only supervised-- supervisor.
And probably with him, she became pregnant.
She somehow-- I don't know why-- she was hiding.
But six months, she came to me.
I say, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all right, we will have to terminate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was not-- not an abortion.
That I had to induce labor.
Oh, that is-- I dilated the cervix.
I ruptured the membranes.
And I had between-- [INAUDIBLE] to stimulate the uterus.
And I brought to-- she had contractions.
She had labor.
And she expelled the fetus.
The fetus was-- I don't-- you don't care.
I don't care what it is.
No, it is--
When it weighs 600 grams, you know, what do I care?
I am in a concentration camp.
I know the results.
I know the results that she-- if the baby is alive, and she's alive, they both will be killed.
I don't know what my sanitator, you know, did, you know?
Probably went somewhere and buried-- buried them in the premises of this-- of this-- compound.
Hold on, I didn't finish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was starting to get a little temperature.
And I decided to keep here for one, two days.
I didn't know what is the reason for the temperature.
Maybe she has some TB in the lungs, you know, and a temperature.
But on the second day, or on the third day, I-- someone from the camp comes running to me, Visner is here.
Visner is-- we are now a part of a concentration camp, Riga [NON-ENGLISH] Kaiserwald.
We are a part.
That's when the SS people came.
That is everything part of the concentration.
But we were Arbeitslager.
We were working, working, working for their very important part.
They don't have any other workers but Jews to work on the airport to enlarge and maintain.
And Visner was the-- there was-- the doctor of this Kaiserwald was a man with the name Krepsbach.
He was in the rank of major in SS, Sturmbannfuhrer-- Sturmbannfuhrer Krepsbach, nickname Dr.
Spritzbach.
Spritz, you know, what means-- injection.
He was known-- he was before this, he was in Mauthausen-- Camp Mauthausen.
And he was with terrible reputation came.
He was injecting phenol intravenously, and the terrible pains, which in ten minutes, they were dead.
What?
